direction_name: Silence or underspecification around boundary cases
where_to_look: Sections that define normal values, normal message flow, or common cases while leaving malformed, extreme, out-of-range, or exceptional cases unspecified.
reverse_inference_rule: If accepting the abnormal case would break a stated protocol goal, infer a candidate constraint requiring explicit rejection, bounded handling, or consistent normalization.
applicability_boundary: Applies when the abnormal case can be tied to a protocol goal such as parser termination, identity preservation, replay prevention, or interoperability.
false_positive_risk: Some standards intentionally leave error handling implementation-defined; such cases require L1/L2 strength review.

where_to_look: The same entity, field, state, capability, or behavior described in multiple sections with inconsistent scope, strength, or boundary.
reverse_inference_rule: Infer a candidate constraint requiring consistent interpretation, or requiring the implementation to follow the stronger safety-preserving interpretation.
applicability_boundary: Applies when an implementation must choose one interpretation and the weaker choice can break a protocol goal.
false_positive_risk: The result may be a standard ambiguity rather than an implementation defect; keep ambiguity as a separate outcome.

where_to_look: Text saying an implementation may allow, support, accept, or extend something without defining the extent of that support.
reverse_inference_rule: Infer that partial support must preserve protocol goals; unsupported cases should be rejected explicitly rather than silently truncated, corrupted, or mis-mapped.
applicability_boundary: Applies when the implementation advertises or accepts a capability and then handles only part of its semantic range.
false_positive_risk: MAY-level language can weaken defect claims; bind the constraint to a concrete protocol goal before using it as an oracle.

where_to_look: Fields or mechanisms whose purpose is stated, such as identity, correlation, integrity, anti-replay, downgrade prevention, or cache isolation, while the required implementation steps remain implicit.
reverse_inference_rule: Infer the minimum behavior needed to preserve the stated purpose, such as uniqueness, request-response association, parser termination, or trust-boundary preservation.
applicability_boundary: Applies when the purpose is explicit enough that violating it would clearly break protocol semantics.
false_positive_risk: The inferred mechanism may be too strong; review whether alternative compliant implementations can preserve the same goal.
